After a tragedy splits the heroes, the world slides into a “peace at any cost” regime, and the ones who refuse to submit are pushed into a brutal fight to reclaim what their symbols used to stand for. Alliances shift, lines get crossed, and the conflict becomes a civil war between icons where every win comes with a price. It's a two-player arcade fighting game built on DC characters, and this version is designed around physical collectible cards. Each play vends a card, and you scan your cards to build a three-character team and trigger bonuses and special attacks through specific combinations.

Stand users from different eras are thrown into the same hunt, where every clash is a mind game and the arena itself turns hostile if you hesitate. Each fighter brings their own tricks and tempo, and the only rule that matters is lasting longer than everyone else. It's a third-person action battle royale built for arcades, supporting up to 20 players per match, with a cabinet designed around dual-handle controls and a touchscreen used for menus.

A security team is sent to Isla Nublar to bring a collapsing park back under control while volcanic activity pushes the island toward disaster. The job is to track down and secure dangerous specimens before the situation becomes unrecoverable, all while keeping fellow team members safe when attacks start coming in waves. It's a two-player light-gun rail shooter split into missions and capped by major boss encounters. The cabinet also exists in a motion-seat edition.

A coalition of super‑villains threatens the planet, and the members of the Justice League join forces to stop the destruction. Players take control of their favorite DC heroes across a series of cooperative missions, from Metropolis to Apokolips, in a battle to save the world.

Five girls from a school light music club meet up after class to rehearse, play together, and turn each song into a small stage moment. It keeps a warm, easygoing vibe built around performances, changing outfits, and that feeling of getting better as a group, where the real challenge is simply staying in time. It's an arcade rhythm game inspired by a PSP title, built around songs from the anime and a collectible card system. You obtain cards and use them to trigger special character voiceovers and change how the band looks in-game, all as a licensed K-ON! adaptation featuring the original cast.

In 2012, Sega unveiled a new arcade combat racing game called K.O. Drive at the DEAL 2012 trade show in Dubai. The game later appeared at a few other exhibitions but was ultimately cancelled, as it never managed to find a gameplay formula that was truly fun to play. It was developed by Sega's Chinese studio, the same team behind the quirky shooter The Golden Gun, which later went on to work on Transformers: Human Alliance, Daytona Championship USA, and other arcade titles.

A falling object puzzle with a sweets motif of beautiful girls! -A falling block puzzle game in which cute beautiful girls appear and play against each other with a puzzle with the motif of “sweets”. If you arrange three sweets that fall from the top of the screen side by side, the sweets will disappear. When you hit the bottles against each other, the sweets in the bottle will break and appear. When it breaks, a chain occurs in eight directions. You can play in either the one-player mode or the two-player mode, which is tailored to the story. -Cotton, the main character of the popular “Cotton Rock and Roll”, has also appeared and is used as a player character.

At a magic academy built around a coveted relic, rivals gather around the same board and the chase begins. One player holds the key, the others hunt them down, and the match turns into a tense tug-of-war where timing, positioning, and a well-played trick can flip the whole table. It's a competitive arcade title for up to four players that blends board-style movement with card-driven battles, running on Sega's Triforce hardware. The cabinet uses physical cards and an IC-card profile system to support collection and progression.

Following a temporal distortion, several King of Fighters warriors are transported to a celestial realm overrun by mystical entities. The heroes must harness their powers to defeat these threats in the skies and find a way back to the human world. A shoot'em up spin‑off of the KOF series, Sky Stage features Kyo, Iori, Terry, Mai, Athena, and Kula in vertical aerial battles against supernatural foes.

Set five years after the events of XI, a new tournament is announced, but it's really a cover for Those From the Past as they try to break Orochi's seal by feeding on the energy of battle. Ash Crimson, the focal point of the storyline that began in The King of Fighters 2003, reaches the end of an arc built on theft, deception, and betrayals. It's a 2D team-based fighter built around three-character squads, and compared with the previous entry it shifts the system toward a more technical, more explosive style by emphasizing meter management and cancel chains, introducing tools like EX Mode and Hyper Drive to extend routes and push combos further.

Four years after the 2010 events, the tournament returns under Antonov, a billionaire who bought the rights and relaunches it as a worldwide spectacle. Veterans and newcomers collide, until the finals spiral out of control when Verse appears and draws in fighters tied to other dimensions. With Ash's storyline already concluded, this entry kicks off a new saga by framing the tournament as the spark for a much larger threat. It's a three-on-three team fighter with 3D character models played on a 2D plane. Compared to the previous game, it leans more into accessibility with Rush Combo, and it reshapes resource management through MAX Mode and Climax Super Special Moves that make combo sequences more explosive.

After The King of Fighters 2001 closed the NESTS saga, this release returns to the 2002 “dream match” idea rather than pushing the canon storyline forward. The point is to throw a huge cast into the same tournament, mix eras freely, and let the rivalry-driven competition take center stage, with Omega Rugal positioned as the ultimate threat. It's a 2D three-on-three team fighter, and compared to the previous entry it drops the Striker format and goes back to classic 3v3. Compared to the original 2002 release, Unlimited Match expands and rebalances the roster and strengthens the system toolset (notably around MAX Activation) for deeper, more tournament-stable play.

game_name: King of Fighters 98, The - The Ultimate Match
synopsis: This version, the first HD adaptation of KOF '98 on Taito Type X, retains the Neo‑Geo original's base while incorporating the expanded content from the PlayStation 2 Ultimate Match release. It features 64 fighters from past entries, along with new stages, remixed music, and an “Ultimate” mode that lets players freely combine the Advanced and Extra systems, bringing the classic up to modern HD arcade standards.

game_name: King of Fighters Maximum Impact Regulation A
synopsis: A 3D spin‑off of the KOF series, Regulation A brings back classic heroes in a new underground world tournament set after the events of Maximum Impact 2. The game pits fighters from various King of Fighters factions against each other and introduces new characters from other SNK franchises.

The first HD KOF on the Taito Type X² system (2009), The King of Fighters XII reimagines the series with hand‑drawn 2D graphics and streamlined 3‑on‑3 gameplay without a main storyline. As a visual and technical reboot, it laid the groundwork for The King of Fighters XIII.

game_name: King of Fighters XIII Climax, The
synopsis: The final chapter of the Ash Crimson saga, this version follows the ultimate confrontation between Elizabeth and Ash against the mysterious Saiki, descendant of an ancient bloodline that controls time. The King of Fighters XIII tournament becomes the stage for the final battle to seal the power of the green flames and restore balance to the world. The King of Fighters XIII: Climax is the definitive and rebalanced edition of the 2010 release. It retains the high‑definition 2D three‑on‑three battles introduced in KOF XII, while refining the gameplay with a complete roster, an expanded EX system, and rebalanced modes.

game_name: King of Fighters XIII Climax, The
synopsis: The final chapter of the Ash Crimson saga, this version follows the ultimate confrontation between Elizabeth and Ash against the mysterious Saiki, descendant of an ancient bloodline that controls time. The King of Fighters XIII tournament becomes the stage for the final battle to seal the power of the green flames and restore balance to the world. The King of Fighters XIII: Climax is the definitive and rebalanced edition of the 2010 release. It retains the high‑definition 2D three‑on‑three battles introduced in KOF XII, while refining the gameplay with a complete roster, an expanded EX system, and rebalanced modes.

In a manga-style pro-wrestling tournament, characters from the Kinnikuman series clash in the ring for the top spot. It's a fast, arcade-driven 3D versus wrestling fighter focused on throws, pacing, and flashy special moves inspired by the manga/anime.

A new manga-style pro-wrestling tournament brings Kinnikuman characters back into the ring, leaning hard on signature throws and over-the-top special techniques straight from the anime. The sequel keeps the same fast, arcade-driven 3D versus wrestling fights, while expanding on the original with a larger selection of wrestlers and more fan-favorite faces in the roster.

The King of Fighters '98: Ultimate Match Final Edition is the definitive version of SNK's “Dream Match”, released in 2011 on Taito Type X via NESiCAxLive. Based on the 2008 edition, it features a comprehensive rebalance, refined timing, and reduced input lag for a more competitive experience. Less focused on visual content, it emphasizes stability and balance, with all characters unlocked, the original Neo‑Geo OST, and NESiCA support for player data—making it the reference version on both arcade and PC.

In a stylized reimagining of the Three Kingdoms era, rival kingdoms push their ambitions forward through personal duels where pride, tactics, and momentum can decide everything. The fighters are based on well-known figures from the period, reinterpreted with a bold anime flair as each side tries to seize the upper hand. On the gameplay side, it's a versus-focused 2D fighter with a strongly grounded pace, built around punishes, counters, and a strategist-style assist system that adds options without overwhelming the match flow

In a highly stylized take on the Three Kingdoms era, rival war leaders settle pride and ambition through one-on-one duels, with an unapologetic anime tone and faction drama driving the matchups. It's a 2D versus fighter built around grounded combat and strategist-style assists that shape pressure and combo routes. Version 3 refreshes the package with updated animations, a button dash, combo tweaks, new additions Xu Huang and Guo Jia, and APM3 features like online play plus Aime-linked player data.

In a noisy arcade setting, it's all about the loop of placing a bet, spinning the reels, and chasing that moment when everything finally lines up. Bonus rounds break up the routine with short, showy sequences, giving you a few extra decisions and a sense that the machine is building toward a payout. On the gameplay side, it's an arcade video slot built around flashy presentation, sound-driven feedback, and bonus/mini-game modes designed to keep the action moving between spins.

When the outbreak hits during a trip in the United States, four survivors are stranded far from home with one goal: push forward together and make it to a safe room before they're overrun. On the gameplay side, it's a four-player co-op arcade FPS built on the foundations of Left 4 Dead 2, but with an all-new survivor cast created for Japanese arcades.

game_name: Let's Go Island - Lost on the Island of Tropi
synopsis: Let's Go Island: Lost On the Island of Tropics is a Sega arcade game and a sequel to Let's Go Jungle!: Lost on the Island of Spice. Taking place on a Pacific island, the protagonists are a goofball New Zealander guide called Zack and a nerdy Canadian girl called Beth. While on a tour, their our boat collides with pirates, leading to encounters with a giant octopus, a bee queen, and a monstrous coral creature. As they navigate these dangers, they must survive the island's challenges.

game_name: Let's Go Island - Lost on the Island of Tropi
synopsis: Let's Go Island: Lost On the Island of Tropics is a Sega arcade game and a sequel to Let's Go Jungle!: Lost on the Island of Spice. Taking place on a Pacific island, the protagonists are a goofball New Zealander guide called Zack and a nerdy Canadian girl called Beth. While on a tour, their our boat collides with pirates, leading to encounters with a giant octopus, a bee queen, and a monstrous coral creature. As they navigate these dangers, they must survive the island's challenges.

A couple joins a tropical island tour hoping to reset their relationship, but the trip derails the moment the jungle starts spitting out mutated creatures. With danger everywhere, they can only push forward and reach safety. On the gameplay side, it's a 1–2 player co-op on-rails shooter built like an arcade ride. The Special version adds a quirky tour guide character, a shorter route-branching structure, QTEs handled through two large buttons, and a dedicated cabinet featuring a full 360° rotating seat plus dual front/back screens.

With danger everywhere, they can only push forward and reach safety. On the gameplay side, it's a 1–2 player co-op on-rails shooter built like an arcade ride. The Special version adds a quirky tour guide character, a shorter route-branching structure, QTEs handled through two large buttons, and a dedicated cabinet featuring a full 360° rotating seat plus dual front/back screens.

game_name: Let's Go Jungle! - Lost on the Island of Spi
synopsis: During a photo safari on a remote island in Asia, Ben and Norah witness their guide being attacked by mutant creatures. To escape the so‑called “Island of Spice,” they must work together to survive, battling hordes of monsters, giant spiders, and carnivorous insects. Their goal: escape alive and save their relationship — literally tested by the game! Let's Go Jungle! is a humorous cooperative rail shooter where teamwork between the two players affects progress and endings. It combines fast‑paced shooting sequences, QTE events, and co‑op mini‑games in a vibrant, tropical adventure from Sega AM1

Let's Go Safari is an interactive children's arcade game developed by Sega, housed in a jeep‑shaped cabinet that seats two players (either two kids or a child and an adult). It simulates a drive through a wildlife park, where players choose a safari type (“baby,” “wild,” or “cute”) and visit three animals out of seven, each shown through short live‑action clips with narrated facts. Gameplay focuses on interactivity — steering the jeep, using the wipers, honking, and changing gears to adjust speed.

In Tokyo, a string of violent incidents spirals from daylight robberies to full-on counter‑terror operations. Each mission puts you in a different law‑enforcement role, pushing you toward the next checkpoint while you deal with threats quickly and avoid hitting bystanders. On the gameplay side, it's a 3D light‑gun rail shooter playable solo or with two players, leaning into a competitive pace where you're racing for the lead. A shield mechanic triggered by aiming off‑screen makes you trade safety for forward momentum.

Two explorers embark on a journey through pyramids, temples, and sunken caverns to uncover the secrets of a lost civilization. But their quest soon turns into a fight for survival, facing ancient traps, mechanical guardians, and mythical creatures. Lost Land Adventure is a co‑op rail shooter in the style of Deadstorm Pirates, blending exploration, treasure hunting, and action shooting. Players take on the roles of two adventurers searching for legendary artifacts hidden within forgotten ruins around the world. The game alternates between intense shooting sequences, light puzzles, and interactive moments such as QTEs, branching paths, and destructible environments.

game_name: House Of The Dead EX, The
synopsis: This spin-off in The House of the Dead series takes a lighter, more mischievous tone. Zobio and Zobiko, two zombies in love, decide to escape Dr. Curien and the creatures he sends after them. Their getaway carries them from one hiding place to the next, through shattered streets and underground corridors, with one simple goal in mind: get far enough away to finally be free. Gameplay-wise, the story unfolds through a string of mini-games where you have to hit score targets within a time limit.

Guide clumsy Luigi through huge ghost-infested mansions on an action-packed ghost hunt just for arcades. Flash your Strobulb at the ghosts to stop them in their tracks and use the Poltergust 5000 to vacuum up the stunned ghost's energy. Once the number above the ghosts head reaches zero, he goes bye-bye! Armed with the ghost-sucking Poltergust 5000 and other new gadgets, not only will you have to capture ghosts, but also solve puzzles and battle monster-sized bosses. Can you and Luigi build up the courage to save the day?
English Synopsis missing

In the near future, the world is engulfed in large‑scale aerial conflicts. The best pilots on the planet are deployed on high‑intensity missions to eliminate enemy forces and restore global peace. At the controls of a fighter aircraft, you face relentless waves of hostile units across missions set around the world. Mach Storm is an immersive arcade flight simulator featuring a 180‑degree dome cockpit derived from the Ace Combat: Assault Horizon Dome of Vision system. Players pilot a futuristic jet through spectacular aerial battles across global hotspots. Its streamlined gameplay, focused on lock‑ons, automatic maneuvers, and intuitive shooting, follows the accessible Ace Combat formula.

A Sensational Experience Combining Drop-Down Puzzle with Beats! The main BGM is composed by the Nico Nico celebrity and sound creator, 'Kikuo'. Last but not least, don't forget the thirteen colorful pixel-art characters to take you through the game.

Maimai is an arcade rhythm game, in which the player interacts with objects on a touchscreen and executes dance-like movements. The game supports both single-player and multiplayer gameplay with up to 4 players. This is the second installment in the maimai franchise.

game_name: Mario and Sonic at the Rio 2016 Olympic Games
synopsis: The heroes of Nintendo and Sega face off at the Rio 2016 Olympic Games. Across events like running, swimming, football, and show jumping, players compete to break records, win medals, and prove who is the true Olympic champion.

game_name: Mario And Sonic At The Tokyo 2020 Olympic Gam
synopsis: The Tokyo 2020 Olympic Games bring together Mario, Sonic, and their friends to compete in various sporting disciplines. Players control iconic characters like Mario, Luigi, Peach, Bowser from Nintendo's side, and Sonic, Tails, Knuckles, Amy from Sega's side. Events include the 100-meter dash, sport climbing, skateboarding, and karate.

where one well-placed shell can flip the race in seconds. The mood stays playful, with drifting, item pickups, and catch-up systems designed to keep the pack close instead of leaving anyone far behind. Its roster blends Nintendo regulars with Namco guests such as Pac-Man and Ms. Pac-Man, in a crossover made for the Triforce arcade platform and linked head-to-head cabinet play.

Under the bright lights of a broadcast-style championship, drivers battle through fast rounds where one mistake can sting and a single item pickup can flip the order instantly. Drifting, boosts, and sneaky hazards keep the pace high, with arcade-friendly races and lively commentary riding along with the action. The roster leans into the crossover, mixing Nintendo regulars with Namco guests like Pac-Man and even Mametchi from Tamagotchi, built for Triforce cabinets and linked versus play for up to four players.

Join characters from the colorful world of the plumber Super Mario, from Waluigi and Yoshi to Donkey Kong and Bowser, plus two guests from Namco: Pac-Man, the favorite arcade mascot, and Don-Chan, the fun hero of the Taiko no Tatsujin rhythm game series. Players can take a photo before each race to personalize their experience on the 42-inch high-definition screen displaying this unique title in the Mario Kart series, which incorporates a 'fusion' mode inherited from the GameCube title Double Dash, allowing for two-player cooperative play on a single kart! Thirty tracks are integrated, including the tracks based on Namco games. The game benefits from the innovations of Mario Kart 7, the work of the 3DS. Over a hundred different items are included in this game; with a random draw of three items at the start of the race.

Over a hundred different items are included in this game; with a random draw of three items at the start of the race.

Spotlights come up on a race built like a live show, with short rounds where position is earned through driving skill and smart item timing. Drifting, shortcuts, and sudden reversals keep the pack tight, especially when multiple cabinets are linked together. Designed for the arcade, it focuses on instant handling, versus and co-op modes, and a crossover roster that blends Nintendo's world with guest characters like Pac-Man and Don-chan.

In a training facility, a run of combat simulations spirals out of control as hostile units seize the scenario. Working together, players must hold their ground, pick targets fast, and stay sharp under pressure. The pace ramps up with waves of enemies and well-timed power-ups, while ammo management keeps every second tense. It's built like a large arcade shooting range with realistic light guns, designed for high replay value and ticket redemption.

Night falls and the city turns into a neon-lit battleground where every straight begs for full throttle and every corner punishes hesitation. Races come one after another like a fast tour through major districts, with traffic, jumps, and rival drivers always close enough to force mistakes. You're not just chasing the finish line, you're trying to keep control when speed and pressure spike at the same time. It's a punchy arcade street racer built around drifting, nitro boosts, and showy hazards, with cabinets that can be linked for head-to-head LAN play.

Sirens cut through the night as suspects race down the highway, trying to outrun the law. Each stage turns into a high-speed pursuit where weaving through traffic and timing your takedowns are key to success. This 3D edition makes use of the Model 3's stereoscopic display system, requiring dedicated 3D glasses and delivering a stronger sense of depth than the standard version. Built for arcades, it focuses on fast action, competitive scoring, and head-to-head play.

In a carnival-style Wild West, a lone gunslinger steps up where every challenge is paid in nerve and timing. The game cycles through rule sets that keep you alert: shooting-gallery style rounds, search-and-shoot moments where spotting the right target matters as much as accuracy, and quick duels that punish hesitation. Unlike straightforward rail shooters, it's built as a medal-driven arcade experience, stacking bonuses, boss encounters, and multiple modes on SEGA's dedicated cabinet hardware.

game_name: Melty Blood - Actress Again - Current Code
synopsis: Melty Blood: Actress Again - Current Code is a 2D fighting game and a spinoff of a visual novel about vampires, Tsukihime. There are 31 characters from the series, each with a moveset based on three types of styles. Current Code is a rebalanced version of the previous Melty Blood release, Actress Again, featuring two new characters but missing the previous game's single-player visual novel modes.

game_name: Melty Blood - Actress Again - Current Code
synopsis: Melty Blood: Actress Again - Current Code is a 2D fighting game and a spinoff of a visual novel about vampires, Tsukihime. There are 31 characters from the series, each with a moveset based on three types of styles. Current Code is a rebalanced version of the previous Melty Blood release, Actress Again, featuring two new characters but missing the previous game's single-player visual novel modes.

An alien threat erupts across the city, and a covert agency sends two agents in before the public catches on. Mission after mission, you clear infested hotspots, survive ambushes, and power up by collecting stronger weapons on the way to a final boss showdown. Runs don't play out the same thanks to changing routes, enemies, and scenarios, and the cabinet leans fully into modern arcade spectacle with two recoil plasma-gun controllers, tight score chasing, and optional ticket redemption.

At the base of a trap-filled castle, a stubborn courier has one goal: reach the top floor before time runs out and deliver the milk to the king. The controls are simple but demanding, move and punch. Your punch doubles as propulsion, letting you bounce off the ground and walls to climb, and every mistake bleeds away precious seconds. The difficulty is intentionally high, yet you pop back in instantly after a fall as long as time remains. As an APM3 arcade title, it can also save progress when you use an Aime card.

In a fantasy Britain where countless “Arthurs” fight to become the one true king, the struggle for Excalibur erupts into all-out duels. Warriors from other worlds join the fray, turning each match into a clash of personalities and styles as much as a test of execution. It's a fast 2D fighting game built around a main character backed by three selectable support knights you can call mid‑pressure or mid‑combo, with power-up states and flashy supers designed for competitive arcade play on a networked platform.

Field agents are deployed globally to sabotage a terrorist plot before a devastating weapon is triggered. The action is relentless, blending firefights against robotic waves with high-pressure hacking sequences. Players use a specialized dual-trigger light gun to swap between primary fire and spy gadgets on the fly. The journey spans nine stages across three major missions.

game_name: Mobile Suit Gundam - Bonds Of The Battlefield
synopsis: Set in the heart of the Universal Century war, you step into a panoramic cockpit for battles where tactics matter more than raw reflexes. This is not just a straightforward shooter, each match requires you to pick a role and coordinate with your squad to control the battlefield. A lone pilot rarely lasts long, so positioning, support, and timing are key. The P.O.D. cabinet enhances the immersion with hand controls and foot pedals that mimic real piloting, while a personal pilot card tracks your rank and progression between sessions.

Step into the arena for intense two-on-two battles drawn from the vast history of the Gundam saga. Victory is not just about reflexes, but about strong coordination with your partner. Each unit carries a specific cost value, and when it is destroyed, it reduces the team's shared life gauge. Choosing a high-cost mobile suit means taking a greater risk if you make a mistake. The gameplay emphasizes awareness, positioning, and matchup knowledge across a large roster of machines, all presented with visuals faithful to the original animation.

game_name: Mobile Suit Gundam - Extreme Versus 2 XBoost
synopsis: In fast-paced arena battles, two mobile-suit pairs clash where spacing, positioning, and teamwork decide the outcome. Your unit choice matters because the cost system makes a high-cost loss far more punishing for the team. This version mainly expands coordinated play with new EX Bursts and the EX Burst Cross mechanic, which adds extra effects when both partners burst together. With 198 units drawn from 41 Gundam works and many returning suits reworked, it's a 2v2 versus title built for competitive arcade play.

game_name: Mobile Suit Z-Gundam - A E U G
synopsis: Eight years after the One Year War, the conflict between AEUG and the Titans escalates, and every operation quickly turns into mobile suit combat. You pick a side, a pilot, and a machine, then dive into arena-style battles where reading the battlefield matters as much as aim. The game is built around two‑on‑two teamwork: covering your partner, avoiding isolation, and choosing the right moment to finish an enemy. In arcades you can play solo with an AI teammate, or face other players depending on the cabinet setup.

game_name: Mobile Suit Z-Gundam - A E U G
synopsis: Eight years after the One Year War, the conflict between AEUG and the Titans escalates, and every operation quickly turns into mobile suit combat. You pick a side, a pilot, and a machine, then dive into arena-style battles where reading the battlefield matters as much as aim. The game is built around two‑on‑two teamwork: covering your partner, avoiding isolation, and choosing the right moment to finish an enemy. In arcades you can play solo with an AI teammate, or face other players depending on the cabinet setup.

On a Grand Prix bike, you fight through a flat-out pack, leaning hard to hold your line and drive cleanly out of corners. The goal is straightforward, but there's little room for error: miss a braking point and the race can slip away. You run across ten official circuits, with three difficulty modes that noticeably change the handling. The cabinet leans into immersion with a ride-on bike, a front-mounted fan for wind effect, a front-facing camera, and CoinUp online features for stat tracking and races of up to eight linked riders, locally or across locations.

game_name: MS Gundam SEED Destiny O.M.N.I. VS Z.A.F.T
synopsis: The war escalates in the Cosmic Era, and clashes between the Alliance and ZAFT leave little room for improvisation. You pick a faction, a pilot, and a mobile suit, then enter arena battles built around distance control, angles, and constant support play. Team matches often revolve around two‑on‑two: cover a pressured partner, isolate a target, and finish cleanly before momentum flips. As a follow-up, it keeps the core formula while expanding the roster and refining key systems like unit cost and multiple awakening styles, pushing matches toward faster, more tactical exchanges.

game_name: MS Gundam SEED Destiny O.M.N.I. VS Z.A.F.T
synopsis: The war escalates in the Cosmic Era, and clashes between the Alliance and ZAFT leave little room for improvisation. You pick a faction, a pilot, and a mobile suit, then enter arena battles built around distance control, angles, and constant support play. Team matches often revolve around two‑on‑two: cover a pressured partner, isolate a target, and finish cleanly before momentum flips. As a follow-up, it keeps the core formula while expanding the roster and refining key systems like unit cost and multiple awakening styles, pushing matches toward faster, more tactical exchanges.

On a music-themed planet, sound monsters pour onto the screen and you have to stop them without breaking the beat. The concept blends shooting with rhythm play: you aim with a gun controller, but scoring comes from firing in time with the on-screen timing markers tied to the song. In co-op, each player follows their own color while sharing the clear gauge, so coordination matters more than spraying shots. This entry refreshes the series with a new visual style, additional play modifiers (mirror, sudden, etc.), and a large set of tracks.

At night, Palm City turns into a hunting ground. Street racers push their luck through high-speed runs, bait rivals into brutal clashes, and try to shake off increasingly aggressive cops across stunt-heavy routes. One mistake can cost the race, but a well-timed boost can flip the outcome in an instant.

Inside a bright, target-filled arena packed with moving props and reactive set pieces, players compete in a fast-paced test of aim and reflexes. Using blasters modeled after NERF toys, they chain accurate shots to trigger score multipliers, unlock bonuses and push their totals higher across a range of lively stages. Designed for one to four participants in cooperative or competitive play, the experience delivers short, energetic sessions built around precision shooting. Developed by Raw Thrills under official license from Hasbro, it brings the NERF brand into a colorful, family-friendly arcade format.

game_name: New Super Mario Bros Wii Coin World
synopsis: In the Mushroom Kingdom, Bowser and his minions have scattered treasures and coins across a series of fast, unpredictable challenges. Players take control of Mario, Luigi and friends to tackle these varied mini-games, combining luck and skill to collect as many medals as possible. Each challenge reflects the bright, familiar world of the Mario license, complete with iconic enemies and items. Developed by Capcom under license from Nintendo, the game turns the Mario adventure into a fun, competitive arcade experience for players of all ages.

A racing game exclusive to Nickelodeon for arcades. It features characters and tracks from SpongeBob SquarePants, The Fairly OddParents, The Adventures of Jimmy Neutron: Boy Genius, Danny Phantom, Avatar: The Last Airbender, and Invader Zim, as well as an original villain character named Lord Nitro.

In a world ruled by darkness, vampires, zombies, demons, and cursed statues roam freely. A fearless hunter sets out to track them down and restore order. Armed with a cannon that can switch ammo types to suit each enemy, he moves through eerie environments where quick reflexes and precision are key. With fast-paced action, an intense atmosphere, and intuitive controls, this rail shooter delivers an engaging experience for one or two players in cooperative or competitive play, developed by Universal Space.

Under the cover of night, riders push their bikes to the limit along crowded city streets, aiming to outpace rivals and tame every sharp turn. With a choice of motorcycles that each handle differently, pilots must use momentum and traffic patterns to stay ahead. The experience blends high‑speed excitement with the challenge of avoiding obstacles while managing boost and control. Built for arcade cabinets with handlebar controls and a dynamic seat, this racing game focuses on pure competition and skillful riding.

An interdimensional distortion brings together the heroines of Nitroplus and partner series such as Saya no Uta, Demonbane, Phantom of Inferno, Kikokugai: The Cyber Slayer, Fate/Stay Night, Blassreiter, and Psycho‑Pass. Guided by Nitroplus‑chan, they must confront an entity seeking to absorb their powers and erase reality itself. Nitroplus Blasterz: Heroines Infinite Duel is a fast‑paced 2D crossover fighting game focused on speed, timing, and cooperation between main characters and support partners. Its system emphasizes offensive chains, assist summons, and spectacular Burst Attacks, in the spirit of classic anime‑style fighters.

After vampire hunters destroy her home, Lilinor sets out with her loyal companion Zasha to find a new stronghold. Deep in the woods, the ruins of an old castle look like salvation, until they realize demons have already claimed it. The journey unfolds as a tough, retro-styled 2D stage-clearing action game with puzzle-like platforming, where learning enemy patterns and timing is key.

Two drivers battle for position across rough off-road courses filled with mud, cliffs and unforgiving corners where a single mistake can spin you out. Winning is about controlling the slide, recovering fast and timing your nitro to steal crucial meters. Built for arcades, it delivers a loud, physical ride with a force-feedback wheel, pedals and a cockpit-style cabinet, continuing Midway's “Thunder” line of spectacle-driven racers.

At summer festivals across Japan, a busy takoyaki stall hustles to serve waves of customers craving hot, savory octopus balls. Behind the grill, players must swiftly manage ingredients and timing, turning batter into perfectly cooked takoyaki while maximizing customer satisfaction. Success depends on quick reflexes and strategic choices as orders pile up and the pace quickens.

It's 2035 and the war on terror continues. The terrorist network known as the Blue Wolf is on the rise and they have seized uranium in order to build their own nuclear weapons. The Liberals form a task force to exterminate the Blue Wolf, recover the uranium and stop a nuclear catastrophe. The task force is called G.H.O.S.T. (Global Humanitarian Operations & Special Tactics). Operation Ghost puts players in the shoes of highly skilled special forces operatives. They must use precision shooting and quick reactions to complete the mission, while making use of some of the brand new tools at their disposal.

Orange County Choppers is an arcade pinball game developed by Incredible Technologies, based on the hit TV show American Chopper. The playfield captures the iconic workshop atmosphere, featuring signature visuals and sounds (bikes like the Black Widow or Jet Bike, voice clips from Paul Sr. and Paul Jr.). The gameplay stays true to classic pinball mechanics, but the themed details and ambiance immerse players in the show's competitive, family-driven spirit.

Otomedius is a quirky arcade shooter where female pilots embody Konami's iconic ships (Vic Viper, Lord British, etc.). The game blends fast-paced action, humor, and parody, with classic Gradius gameplay—level selection, special attacks, and touchscreen targeting. Set in a tokusatsu-inspired universe, it pits Angel Squadrons against the Gofer Sisters, all wrapped in Mine Yoshizaki's signature manga style.

Otoshu DX is a hybrid arcade game blending bullet hell and rhythm mechanics. Players face side-scrolling enemy waves, interspersed with multi-lane rhythm sequences. Based on the 2019 mobile release, the arcade version features a wider screen, extra tracks, and demanding gameplay that rewards precision and timing.

OutRun 2 SP SDX is an updated version of OutRun 2 and OutRun 2 SP. It stands for 'OutRun 2 Special Tours Super Deluxe' and was first shown at a privately held Sega show on July 7, 2006. This version of OutRun 2 does not run on the Sega Chihiro, but rather the Sega Lindbergh, allowing the graphics to be displayed at a resolution of 800x480 rather than the previous versions' 640x480. The game features large replica Ferrari cockpits which move and tilt with the onscreen action, and features a cooperative play mode where two players can sit next to each other in the same car and take turns driving with their own set of controls. Two versions of the cabinet were produced. The 2-Player cabinet is designated as DX (Deluxe), and the 4-Player cabinet is designated as SDX (Super Deluxe), with raceview cameras on the players, and a live leaderboard over the center units. These large, expensive SDX units are designed for large game centers and amusement parks as a special attraction.

Pac-Man Battle Royale reinvents the classic by turning the maze into a competitive arena where up to four colored Pac-Men battle to be the last one standing. The goal is no longer just to eat dots, but to eliminate opponents by gobbling them up after eating a power pellet, while avoiding ghosts.

Pac-Man's Arcade Party is an arcade compilation featuring 13 classic Namco games from the 1980s, including Pac-Man, Pac-Mania, Galaga, Dig Dug, Rally-X, and Xevious. Released to celebrate Pac-Man's 30th anniversary, this cabinet lets players relive iconic titles from the golden age of arcades, with faithful graphics and gameplay.

Pac-Man Chomp Mania is a redemption arcade version of the classic Pac-Man, where the player must survive a single round with only one life. Placed in a maze with no dots, aggressive ghosts chase Pac-Man, and the goal is to navigate him into the Ghost Home to win a prize. The cabinet dispenses tickets (up to 1000) or gift cards, and features a vibrant LED marquee and modern design

Haunted Museum is a co-op on-rails shooter where two agents explore a haunted museum where exhibits come to life and attack them. The game features diverse environments (armor, sharks, giant toys) and epic boss battles, all wrapped in a mix of horror and humor. Players must survive waves of enemies by aiming and shooting with precision.

Pengo! Online is a modern, competitive take on the classic Pengo (1982), adapted for the APM3 system. The game features a battle royale mode where up to 8 players compete by pushing ice blocks to crush opponents or score points within a time limit.

game_name: P4U - Persona 4 The Ultimate In Mayonaka Aren
synopsis: Persona 4 Arena throws players into the mysterious 'P-1 Grand Prix' tournament, set in the Midnight Channel's alternate world two months after Persona 4. The Persona 4 Investigation Team and surviving SEES members (Persona 3) clash to uncover the secrets of Labrys, a mysterious android, and the red fog threatening Inaba. The game blends visual novel storytelling with fast-paced 2D fighting, featuring unique character playstyles, mid-battle Persona summons, and accessible yet deep mechanics, signature of Arc System Works.

game_name: Persona 4 - The Ultimax Ultra Suplex Hold
synopsis: Shortly after the events of Persona 4 Arena, the world of Inaba is engulfed in a supernatural red fog. The heroes of Persona 3 and Persona 4 find themselves trapped in a mysterious televised tournament, where their alter egos and Personas battle under the influence of the enigmatic Kagutsuchi. Their goal: uncover the truth behind this new distortion before it consumes reality itself.

Phantom Breaker: Another Code is an arcade version of the 2D fighting game Phantom Breaker, running on Sega's RingEdge 2 system. The game features a unique combat system with two play styles (Quick and Hard), a diverse roster of weapon-wielding characters, and competitive versus mode. This version adds the final boss Infinity as a playable character, rebalanced gameplay, and a new opening movie.

Pink Panther: Jewel Heist is a ticket redemption arcade game based on the iconic Pink Panther cartoon and movie series. Players control the Pink Panther as he descends 100 floors of a building, dodging Inspector Clouseau's patrols and alarms, while collecting coins and the final jewel to win the ticket jackpot.

game_name: Pleasant Goat And Big Big Wolf - Happy Happy
synopsis: The game features four mini-games: Spot Goat , Hide & Seek , Math Play , and Spot Difference . Throughout the games, players may encounter Wolffy, who disrupts gameplay and can be tapped to be chased away for extra points. Achieving target scores in any mini-game grants access to bonus game, which involve quick reactions to thwart the wolves' actions.

In the Ferrum Region Battle Tournament, Trainers team up with their Pokémon to prove their strength and reach the top of the championship. As they progress, they uncover the existence of a Pokémon corrupted by a mysterious energy — Shadow Mewtwo, the source of an imbalance threatening the entire battle system. Pokkén Tournament combines the fluid fighting style of Tekken with the signature moves of Pokémon, set within circular 3D arenas. Gameplay alternates between free‑roaming field battles and close‑quarters duels, blending arcade accessibility with strategic depth.

Police Trainer 2 is a light gun shooter arcade game, sequel to the classic Police Trainer (1996). Players take on the role of a police trainee and must complete a series of tests (precision shooting, bomb defusing, suspect neutralization) to earn promotions. The game features light guns, cooperative 2-player mode, progressive difficulty, and a limited life system

At the heart of the story is a powerful clan-turned-conglomerate where leadership is not decided by ballots or quiet inheritance, but by a fighting tournament that crowns the next head of the family. Pride, greed, and long-running grudges turn every match into a personal score to settle, carried by a cast of eccentric relatives and opportunists willing to do anything to claim the name and the throne. Staying true to the Power Instinct lineage, this entry brings the series back to the arcade with a highly expressive 2D fighter built for versus play, mixing over-the-top character comedy with showy mechanics such as transformations.

Across postcard-like settings, each mini-golf hole turns into a small problem to solve, where reading slopes and planning rebounds matters as much as touch and nerve. The goal is simple, but the best scores come from clever lines, controlled power and learning how the course wants the ball to move.

Every hole is a bite-sized puzzle of slopes, rebounds and risk management, where a safe line can be smart but a bold bank shot can win the round. The 2013 edition greatly expands the tour with five new 18-hole courses, bringing the total to eleven, and leans harder into arcade progression by unlocking new outfits and achievement badges through play. A new Poker Putt mode reshuffles what “a good hole” means, while LIVE connectivity adds stat tracking and daily contests; for adult venues, an optional RawTalk commentary setting can be enabled.
Pride GP Grand Prix 2003 is a fighting game with a mix of sports game and wrestling simulator from the developers at Capcom studio.

Primeval Hunt is a first-person light gun shooter where players travel back in time to hunt 10 dinosaur species (T-Rex, Velociraptor, etc.) across 12 levels. The game uses light guns with built-in speakers and a touchscreen for free-roaming navigation. Dinosaurs have weak points (eyes, heart) and will attack if provoked.

Hatsune Miku: Project DIVA Arcade Future Tone is an arcade rhythm game developed by Sega AM2, released in 2013 on the Sega Nu system. Players follow the rhythm of hundreds of Vocaloid songs by pressing on‑screen symbols synchronized with the music, set against dynamic 3D music videos. This version features Full HD visuals, a tactile slide panel, and a massive song library, serving as the foundation for the console release Project DIVA Future Tone DX.

In the year 2012, Psychiccers—humans with psychic abilities—are hunted by governments for their military potential. The story revolves around three conflicting factions: Neo-NOA, seeking a peaceful utopia under their rule; the Army, led by Richard Wong, aiming for global domination through force; and a third force searching for their missing loved ones. Battles take place in zero-gravity cubic arenas, where each warrior wields elemental powers (fire, water, wind, etc.) and psychic techniques to emerge victorious. Gameplay features a 2.5D aerial combat system with free movement in all directions. Players can deploy barriers, perform dashes, and launch ranged attacks while managing their psychic power gauge.

Puck Off turns the classic game of shuffleboard into a fast-paced arcade challenge, where players compete to slide their puck farther than their opponent on a virtual table. The game features three distinct modes (classic, timed, and special challenges) and 8 animated opponents with varying difficulty levels. Using a high-precision trackball, players control the power and direction of their shots, with realistic physics adding to the strategy.

In a modern urban setting, two cops, Sian McHale and Becky Nilson, investigate a terrorist conspiracy threatening the city. Their mission takes them through diverse locations—from downtown streets to the docks—as they neutralize armed enemies to uncover the plot. The game unfolds across three chapters (a hotel, an enemy building, and a cargo ship), each featuring intense shooting phases and cinematic action sequences.

Puyo Puyo eSports is a competitive arcade version of the classic puzzle game, designed for tournaments and players seeking a fast, strategic, and no-nonsense experience. The game uses the iconic rules of Puyo Puyo 2 and Puyo Puyo Fever, with refined chain and counterattack mechanics for tense, balanced gameplay. There's no story mode—this is all about local or online duels, with a ranking system inspired by eSports tournaments.

Puyo Puyo!! Quest Arcade was described as a 'real-time cooperative puzzle RPG'.Puyo battles were played using the touch panel on the arcade cabinet. Quests could include up to 4 different players, and play was possible with country-wide (online play) and in-store (local play) teammates. Quests varied in type, including 'story quests', 'subquests', 'event quests', and 'emergency quests'. Similar to Puyo Puyo!! Quest, your team (termed 'party') could be customized and rearranged. Additionally, you could also power up your cards, and transform them to higher rarities.

This high‑definition edition of the original Puzzle Bobble retains the classic 2D sprites with a modernized interface and 720p presentation. It features smoother aiming, a combo scoring system, and a competitive versus mode, while staying true to the original gameplay.

Quake: Arcade Tournament Edition is a rare and innovative arcade port of the legendary FPS Quake (1996), designed for arcades and gaming centers like Sega GameWorks. Unlike the PC version, this port focuses on local multiplayer matches (up to 4 linked cabinets), featuring modified maps and a ticket redemption system for prizes. Players take on the role of a soldier battling demonic creatures in dark arenas, with a hybrid arsenal inspired by Quake and Quake II.
English Synopsis missing

The game focuses on the import scene and illegal street racing. The game runs on the Sega Lindbergh platform, and uses Virtua Fighter 5's characters model engine. Players can save their progress and records by using IC cards. There are boost buttons on the steering wheel, players can use unlimited boosts to speed up during the game.

Players can save their progress and records by using IC cards. There are boost buttons on the steering wheel, players can use unlimited boosts to speed up during the game.

The Rabbids, those zany extraterrestrial rabbits from the Rayman universe, invade Hollywood to wreak havoc across three movie sets: Wild West, Zombie Invasion, and Sci-Fi World. In this cooperative arcade shooter, players use optical guns with plungers (instead of barrels, for safety) to aim and blast the Rabbids, who pull off endless silly antics. Each set features three levels, with power-ups to grab and leaderboards to dominate among friends.

In Raccoon Rampage, players take on the role of mischievous raccoons armed with high-powered water blasters, wreaking havoc across levels packed with quirky enemies and giant bosses. The game offers a cooperative or competitive adventure through diverse environments, where players must collect power-ups, upgrade weapons, and coordinate attacks to defeat over 30 unique enemy types with distinct behaviors. Designed for 2 or 4-player arcade cabinets, Raccoon Rampage features simple yet tactical gameplay, vibrant graphics, and a ticket redemption system to appeal to players of all ages. The mounted water guns spray real streams of water at the screen, creating a unique and immersive experience.

Race Driver: GRID (Arcade) is an adapted version by Sega Amusements of the Codemasters title, released in 2009 on the Sega Europa‑R system. The game delivers dynamic and cinematic races across urban and international circuits, featuring accessible handling and multiplayer support for up to six linked cabinets. Optimized for arcade play, it retains the visual flair and vehicle damage of the original GRID while offering faster, more action‑focused gameplay.

Radikal Bikers is set in a Mediterranean environment, and is based on delivering pizza on a rare Italian scooter called Italjet Dragster in heavy traffic before your AI opponent does, while getting points. The arcade version has three difficulty levels, which correspond to each of the different places: Margherita (easy, set in Milan), Capricciosa (medium, set in Rome), and Diabola (hard, set in Naples). If you beat all four races in a level, you get to play the next level free. Meanwhile, the PlayStation version includes four more locations on top of the original three as part of the extended Radikal mode: Marinara (set in Venice), Fantasia (set in Paris), Reggiana (set in London) and Americana (set in New York)

Raiden III shares the same mechanics as the previous games in the series, while introducing new mechanics to set it apart from its predecessors. Players are given a new primary weapon, the piercing Proton Laser, and a new missile weapon, the Radar Missile. New mechanics introduced in this game include the Flash Shot multiplier, which increases score the faster an appearing enemy is destroyed, and Double Play, which allows one player to control both ships with one controller. Raiden III is the first game in the series to give the player craft smaller hitboxes. The bombs were changed to deploy instantly and cover the entire screen, as opposed to the earlier games' delayed explosion and smaller area of damage. The game consists of seven stages of increasing difficulty, with the first three levels taking place on Earth and the last four taking place in space.

The Crystals have returned again after numerous defeats against humanity. The VCD immediately launches a new model of the Raiden fighter, the Fighting Thunder ME-02 Kai, to stop the Crystals from taking over the Earth.

Rainbow Bombergirl is a competitive MOBA spin-off of the classic Bomberman series, where two teams of 4 'Bombergirls' battle to destroy each other's base. The characters are anime-style 'bishōjo moe' girls, each with unique roles (bomber, blocker, shooter, etc.) and customizable outfits (unlocked via gacha and ranks). The game plays on a classic grid but adds MOBA mechanics: wave management, defense towers, and strategic objectives. The Rainbow update brings enhanced graphics, new characters (including crossovers with Konami franchises like Castlevania), and a live announcer system to hype matches.

The player assumes the role of John Rambo, fighting through five levels. The first and final levels are based on the desert battle from Rambo III, the second and third levels focus on Rambo rescuing the prisoners from Rambo: First Blood Part II, and the fourth level consists of Rambo rescuing his mentor, Col. Trautman, from an enemy prison (also from Rambo III). Each level consists of the player shooting down waves after waves of enemy soldiers; if the player waits too long to kill an enemy, the enemy will attack and the player will lose one of their lives. Some enemies attack Rambo with grenades or rockets, which can be shot and destroyed in mid-air. In addition, the game also features non-shooting segments, such as defeating an enemy in hand-to-hand combat, sneaking past enemies without being spotted, and shooting targeted areas without missing. Failing to complete these segments properly results in the player either losing a life or having to fight more enemies. Every level includes a boss fight, such as the Soviet attack helicopter from Rambo III or the gunboats from Rambo: First Blood Part II, which require the player to avoid a series of attacks while waiting for an opportunity to damage and ultimately defeat the boss.

This modern reboot of Rampage ties into the 2018 film Rampage: Rampage, featuring updated 3D graphics and revamped gameplay. Players control George (giant gorilla), Ralph (werewolf), and Lizzie (monstrous lizard), now with three-stage evolution levels per monster, cinematic destruction physics, and competitive scoring. The goal remains the same: smash cities (including Chicago, inspired by the film), crush buildings, eat civilians for power-ups, and battle the military. The arcade cabinet features HD visuals, classic arcade controls, and over-the-top special effects (explosions, debris physics).

Rastan Saga (NESiCAxLive) is the modern re-release of Taito's legendary 1987 arcade classic. In this iconic game, players take on the role of Rastan, a muscular and fearless barbarian, as he battles through dark forests, labyrinthine dungeons, and forgotten temples filled with the worst creatures of Greek mythology. Armed with his sword, he must jump, climb, and fight legendary foes like cyclopes, dragons, and evil sorcerers, all while collecting magical items—some of which may be cursed, turning the game into a nightmare.

For years, the Toy-Volt company has been producing toys and games for children all over the world. Within months of introducing their first products, Toy-Volt shot to the top! No one could put their finger on exactly what it was that made the toys so popular. Sure, they employed the best designers and marketers in the world, but there was something else about Toy-Volt toys, something almost.... magic. Now, their newest R/C cars really start to live! Re-Volt is a racing game where you take control over one of more than 28 R/C cars and drive on one of the 13 tracks. The tracks are varied, and include a toy shop, a supermarket, a luxury liner and more. It is possible to play a 'reversed' (backwards) or 'mirror' (reversed left and right) variety of each track. There are also 4 levels of realism to choose from.

Set in a futuristic amusement park, Ring Riders throws players into a high-speed motocross race where the goal is to ride through giant rings scattered across diverse tracks while pulling off acrobatic stunts and finishing first.

In the heart of Sherwood Forest, players step into the shoes of the legendary Robin Hood, wielding a life-sized crossbow to test their aim in a series of medieval shooting challenges. The game unfolds in a stylized medieval setting, where the goal is to hit moving targets (sheriff's men, treasure chests, apples, etc.) to rack up points and win redeemable tickets—a staple of arcade prize games.

In 2061, humanity's survival hangs by a thread. BAC, a sentient computer system controlling the world's engines, has turned rogue, halving the global population. A secret faction within Lerman Matsunaga developed the Rolling Gun, a weapon designed to counter BAC's threat. Piloting the RF-42R STORK, players battle through six relentless stages, facing swarms of enemies and screen-filling bosses in a true bullet hell experience. Directed by ex-Cave developer Daisuke Koizumi, it blends retro inspiration with contemporary polish, offering a technical yet accessible experience.

In the near future, the PROBE-NEXUS corporation sponsors the underground Fight for Survival tournament, gathering fighters from Zone Prime's slums. Each character has their own story: the Mutsuki sisters seek revenge against Greed for their father's murder, Zen aims to surpass his rival, and Beatrice, the new PROBE-NEXUS leader, hides darker plans. The game stands out with its strategic combat system, featuring two gauges (offense and defense) for special moves and combos, and stunning 2D visuals powered by S.M.A. animation.

In a post-apocalyptic Tokyo, a mysterious virus turns AKB48 idol members into zombies. The last surviving schoolgirls, still wearing their iconic sailor uniforms, must face their former friends now turned into ravenous undead. Players wield a light gun firing 'antidote bullets' to either cure or eliminate the infected, all while AKB48's pop tunes play in the background. The game blends on-rails shooting with rhythm sections, where players must shoot zombies in time with the music. The over-the-top visuals, giant boss battles, and the arcade cabinet's wind effects create a unique mix of horror, comedy, and idol culture, all wrapped in a vibrant, campy aesthetic

In 1787, during Japan's Tenmei era, the land is ravaged by chaos, famine, and a creeping darkness. Shizuka Gozen, a tormented spirit trapped in the underworld, threatens to destroy everything. Warriors from across the country, each with their own motives, gather to face this evil, including Haohmaru, the wandering swordsman, and Nakoruru, the nature priestess. The game is a return to the series' roots, featuring brutal weapon-based combat, bloody finishing moves, and the iconic 'Rage Gauge' that powers up attacks when the player is near defeat.

Set during Japan's tumultuous Sengoku period, legendary warriors clash in a battle for the nation's fate. Haohmaru, the wandering swordsman, and Nakoruru, the nature priestess, must confront a shadowy conspiracy threatening to rewrite history. Samurai Spirits Sen breaks from tradition with a full 3D combat system, introducing sidestepping, air combos, and horizontal/vertical slashing mechanics. While criticized for straying from the series' 2D roots, the game stands out for its high-definition visuals, a roster of 24 fighters (11 newcomers), and the return of iconic artist Senri Kita. The 'Guard-Shatter Move' adds a tactical layer, allowing players to break through an opponent's defense. Despite mixed reception, it remains a bold experiment in the Samurai Shodown legacy

In a wild prehistoric world, a mother Tyrannosaurus rex sees her clutch of eggs stolen by Oviraptors, minions of an evil shaman. Enraged, she embarks on a relentless quest across hostile landscapes, battling hordes of dinosaurs and fantastical creatures to reclaim her eggs. The game blends frantic action and exploration, with a straightforward yet satisfying combat system : eating foes restores health.

In a world where ten schools battle for global supremacy, each student is backed by a Guardian, a godlike being drawn from Norse mythology. Battles are not just about strength, but strategy: weapon choice, timing, and special skills turn the tide. Players can customize their fighter, select from eight weapon disciplines, and face off in intense 1vs1 duels, either online or via linked arcade cabinets.

Sonic & Sega All-Stars Racing is a racing game developed by Sumo Digital. It stars characters from a wide range of Sega games, similar to the previously released Sega Superstars Tennis, also by Sumo Digital. Unlike Sega Superstars Tennis, Sonic & Sega All-Stars Racing is more skewed towards the Sonic the Hedgehog franchise, with roughly half the tracks based on the series and appoximately one third of the characters.

Sega Race TV is an arcade racing game styled like an American TV show with live commentary. It features five stages, nine licensed cars and characters, and supports both single-player and multiplayer. Races involve eight cars seen from a third-person view, with track traps and a boost mechanic for quick acceleration. In single-player, the goal is to finish top 3, then top 2, and finally 1st across three stages to unlock a character-specific ending movie and receive a password for car customization. Multiplayer supports linking up to four cabinets for eight human racers in a single race where the only goal is to finish first.

Multiplayer supports linking up to four cabinets for eight human racers in a single race where the only goal is to finish first.

Dive into the legendary world of stock-car racing, where speed and precision rule the track. Drivers compete on circuits inspired by classic American ovals, featuring tight turns, blistering straightaways, and spectacular drifts. The game captures the essence of the iconic Daytona USA, now in high definition with a reworked soundtrack to fit modern licensing constraints.

Sega Rally 3 contains three racing modes: World Championship, Quick Race and Classic. World Championship mode is a single player game which takes place across three stages (Tropical, Canyon and Alpine) and largely follows the format of the previous Sega Rally arcade games. The game is a 22 car race played to a time limit, and the player's starting position for one race is determined by their finishing position in the previous race. Unlike the previous games in the series, each race contains two laps. If the player finishes the third stage in first place, they are able to play a head-to-head race on the bonus Lakeside track.

Step into the world of Japanese touring car racing, where drivers battle on legendary tracks like Fuji Speedway, Sugo, and Okayama. Each car, faithfully recreated from the real Super GT series, delivers a unique driving experience, balancing raw power and technical mastery. Races unfold in varied conditions, day and night, with special events and challenges to climb the ranks. The game stands out with its cabinet-linking system, allowing dozens of players to compete simultaneously online or locally. Developed by Sega using Unreal Engine 4, it combines high-definition graphics, realistic physics, and an immersive soundtrack, all under the official Super GT license.

In the tumultuous Sengoku period, Japan's most legendary warlords clash in a no-holds-barred battle for supremacy. Each warrior, armed with supernatural powers and iconic weapons, is a larger-than-life take on historical figures like Date Masamune or Sanada Yukimura. Fights are fast-paced, with lightning-fast combos, devastating special moves, and an assist system that lets players call in an ally to turn the tide.

In a future where humanity has expanded as far as Saturn, 'Rounders' – powerful mechs piloted by humans – have become the ultimate weapons in a silent war between factions. Each pilot, driven by personal motives and backstories, battles in arenas where technology and strategy decide victory. Fights are fast-paced, with rapid-fire exchanges, devastating barrages, and tactical assist calls to turn the tide.

Set against the backdrop of traditional Chinese art and culture, Shanghai IV: Ryuumon Hiyaku immerses players in the timeless world of mahjong solitaire. Each game is a test of focus and strategy, as players match pairs of tiles to clear the board and progress through increasingly complex levels. The tiles, often adorned with dragons, flowers, or Chinese characters, evoke the game's ancient heritage.

A hundred people vanish without a trace, and the investigation leads to an amusement park everyone assumed had been left to rot. Two agents are sent in, expecting to find clues and survivors, only to discover that something has seized control of the place. The rides turn hostile, the pathways become traps, and a twisted carnival cast spills out of the shadows as the case escalates from a search into a fight to get out alive.

The Kingdom of Alcaland, struck by a series of mysterious disappearances and comas, breaks its centuries-old isolation to request urgent aid from Japan. The Association for Psychic Studies dispatches a motley crew of investigators, including exorcists and magicians, to resolve this occult crisis. Each character pursues their own motivations, creating a dynamic narrative that evolves based on the pair of heroes selected by the player. Through a structure blending action and dialogue, the plot unveils the secrets of the royal family and the tensions between the various protagonists. The heroes must face the castle's demons and thwart the plans of dark forces threatening the kingdom to restore peace. This branching scenario offers over fifty variations, making each playthrough a new piece of the narrative puzzle.

In a world where magic and legendary creatures intertwine, heroes of various races (humans, half-beasts, elves, etc.) gather to face dark forces threatening the land. Shining Force Cross (2009) immerses players in a real-time cooperative adventure, where each adventurer is fully customizable—appearance, voice, class, and skills can be tailored to create a unique character. The game unfolds in dungeons brimming with traps, formidable enemies, and hidden treasures. Dynamic and strategic battles require coordination among 1 to 4 players (locally or online) to defeat towering bosses and advance the story.

Shining Force Cross Elysion is a direct sequel to Cross Raid, adding an Elysion mode where players battle enemy waves in open arenas. The game now supports up to 8 simultaneous players, with enhanced graphics, new playable races (such as 'dark races'), and longer epic quests. Combat mechanics are refined for smoother gameplay and deeper team strategies.

Shining Force Cross Exlesia is the fourth iteration of the arcade series, pushing boundaries with massive dungeons, more impressive final bosses, and an even deeper progression system. The game introduces new classes, legendary weapons to collect, and special online events. Graphics and animations are overhauled for maximum immersion, while retaining the cooperative spirit that defined the series.

Shining Force Cross Raid is a major update to Shining Force Cross (2009), introducing a dedicated Raid mode for 6-player groups. Quests are now longer and more strategic, featuring tougher bosses and revamped combat mechanics to encourage team coordination. Players retain character customization, but new classes and skills are added.

In a world where speed and destruction rule, Sega Showdown throws players into frantic races across tracks inspired by real-life locations in the USA and Japan. Drivers compete in a variety of classic and modern cars, navigating through an electric atmosphere where every turn can become an opportunity for an epic overtake… or a spectacular crash. Sega Showdown is an arcade adaptation of Codemasters' DiRT: Showdown, optimized for multiplayer play in arcades, with bonus codes to unlock additional content.

Silent Hill: The Arcade deals with two characters, Eric and Tina, who have entered the town of Silent Hill and must battle monsters while uncovering the mystery behind Eric's nightmares about a girl and a steamship. The gameplay in Silent Hill: The Arcade is similar to the House of the Dead series in which players use light guns to aim and shoot at enemies. Shooting away from the screen reloads the player's gun. The Arcade has a multiplayer element, where each player can choose to play as either Eric or Tina. A second player can join the game at any time as the other character. Progress can be saved by using the e-Amusement pass.

In a world where bowling becomes a national competition, players embody virtual bowlers in realistic alleys inspired by the best bowling centers. Each game is an opportunity to prove their precision, strategy, and consistency, with pins reacting just like in real life. Online tournaments allow players to challenge opponents worldwide, while special modes like 'Spare Challenge' or 'Vegas Mode' (where every strike or spare deals a playing card) add an element of chance and fun.

In 2037, in the corrupt megacity of Freeport, John Blade, commander of HardCorps, is sent to dismantle a mutagenic drug ring orchestrated by SinTEK Industries. The arcade version of SiN adapts this dark, cynical plot into an intense solo experience, where the player battles gangsters, mutants, and robots in detailed environments (banks, sewers, labs). The game stands out with its modified Quake II engine, delivering advanced 3D graphics for its time, realistic physics, and cinematics that enhance immersion. The arcade retains the original's dark humor and sharp dialogue but lacks the multiplayer or online modes found on PC.

In a Dark Deco world, a cursed artifact called the Skull Heart grants the wishes of pure-hearted women—or twists them into monstrous Skullgirls if their soul is impure. Skullgirls 2nd Encore is the definitive version of the game, set after the original's events. Each character's story mode explores a 'what if' scenario where they obtain the Skull Heart, revealing their motivations and flaws. The game stands out with its unique hand-drawn animation style (inspired by 1930s cartoons) and deep, technical gameplay: complex combos, tension mechanics, and team synergies (assists, attack ratios).

In 1996, a cataclysmic plague sweeps across Earth, turning machines and humans into grotesque mutants. You play as the last survivor (or his dog, in co-op mode), piloting the legendary SkyCurser combat vessel to wipe out the threat. The game throws you into a retro post-apocalyptic world, where frantic action and colossal boss battles evoke classic shoot'em ups—but with a modern twist: crisp pixel art, a driving synthwave soundtrack, and deep gameplay mechanics (weapon switching, super attacks, and bullet deflection).

Trolls have escaped from Skylands' prisons and are wreaking havoc! Trigger Happy and the Skylanders need your help to stop them before it's too late. In this arcade and mobile spin-off, players take on the role of a Skylander (chosen from over 30 characters from Spyro's Adventure) and man a flying cannon. The goal? Shoot down trolls, chain combos for high scores, and use magical items (like Anvil Rain or Time Twister) to defeat waves of enemies.

A mysterious mansion traps SNK's iconic heroines in a parallel dimension. Kukri, a shadowy villain, forces them into 2v2 battles to fuel his power. Fighters from The King of Fighters, Fatal Fury, Samurai Shodown, and World Heroes must team up to escape the spell. SNK Heroines: Tag Team Frenzy is a lighthearted, accessible spin-off showcasing SNK's female characters in a whimsical setting. Gameplay simplifies mechanics (one-button special moves) but adds tactical elements like random items and Dream Finish attacks.

An armored superhero must save Earth by punching on-screen targets and enemies using a connected punching bag system. The game features a variety of scenarios: rescuing civilians from danger, battling giant monsters, and defending the planet from catastrophic threats. Sonic Blast Heroes is a modern sequel to the 1990 arcade classic Sonic Blast Man, updating the original's physical punching mechanics with improved safety features and contemporary graphics. Players wear boxing gloves and strike the pad to defeat enemies, trigger special attacks, and progress through increasingly challenging stages—all while immersed in over-the-top superhero action

In a vibrant world inspired by the Sonic the Hedgehog series, players take on the role of Sonic or one of his friends (Tails, Knuckles, Amy, etc.) in a frantic race through iconic environments like beaches, temples, and meadows. The goal? Dodge obstacles, collect as many rings and tickets as possible, and face off against Dr. Eggman in a final boss battle at the end of each level. Sonic Dash Extreme is an arcade adaptation of the mobile game Sonic Dash, designed for arcade halls with a giant 55-inch vertical screen and an oversized D-Pad controller for precise input.

In 1590, after Nightmare's defeat and the apparent destruction of Soul Edge, the cursed sword's fragments draw a new wave of warriors seeking power or redemption. Among them, Kilik and Xianghua, wielders of the holy sword Soul Calibur, attempt to end the curse once and for all. But the spirit of Soul Edge, still alive, corrupts souls and rises again as a new black knight, Inferno, while old rivals like Mitsurugi, Taki, and Siegfried take up arms again for personal reasons.

In 1590, after the battle between Siegfried and Nightmare, the cursed swords Soul Edge and Soul Calibur are sealed together in a fateful embrace. Yet, the spirit of Soul Edge remains active, corrupting souls and seeking a new wielder. Meanwhile, new warriors emerge: Setsuka, a young woman armed with a sword hidden in an umbrella, Zasalamel, a malevolent mage wielding a scythe, and Tira, an acrobat loyal to Soul Edge. Each character has their own motives—whether revenge, power, or breaking the curse.

Space Invaders is a two-dimensional fixed shooter game in which the player controls a laser cannon by moving it horizontally across the bottom of the screen and firing at descending aliens. The aim is to defeat five rows of eleven aliens—some versions feature different numbers—that move horizontally back and forth across the screen as they advance towards the bottom of the screen. The player defeats an alien, and earns points, by shooting it with the laser cannon. As more aliens are defeated, the aliens' movement and the game's music both speed up. Defeating the aliens brings another wave that is more difficult, a loop which can continue indefinitely.

Nico, a spunky girl armed with a magical umbrella, embarks on a surreal adventure through colorful worlds filled with robots, giant toys, and abstract creatures. Her goal: to navigate through trap-filled levels, using her umbrella to jump, attack, and defend herself. Each stage is a race against time, as a giant robot pursues her if she lingers too long. Spica Adventure is a tribute to classic arcade platformers, with a touch of Taito's signature madness.
